exercises.title: Data Inspector
exercises.description: Simulate Pandas `.info()` and `.describe()` on a list of dicts. Write `inspect_data(records)` that prints: total rows, column names with data types, null/missing value counts, and basic statistics for numeric columns. Use a dataset of mixed types including some missing values.
exercises.starterCode:
def inspect_data(records):
    """
    Print a summary of the dataset similar to Pandas .info() + .describe().
    """
    if not records:
        print("Empty dataset")
        return

    print(f"Dataset: {len(records)} rows")
    print()

    columns = list(records[0].keys())
    print("Column Info:")
    print(f"{'Column':<15} {'Type':<10} {'Non-Null':<10} {'Null%':<8}")
    print("-" * 45)

    for col in columns:
        values = [row[col] for row in records]
        # TODO: Count non-null values (None counts as null)
        # TODO: Determine type from first non-null value
        # TODO: Calculate null percentage
        # TODO: Print the row
        pass

    print()
    print("Numeric Column Statistics:")
    for col in columns:
        values = [row[col] for row in records if row[col] is not None]
        if values and isinstance(values[0], (int, float)):
            # TODO: Print mean, min, max for this column
            # Format: "  col: mean=X.X  min=Y  max=Z"
            pass

data = [
    {"name": "Alice",  "age": 28, "salary": 75000, "dept": "Engineering"},
    {"name": "Bob",    "age": None, "salary": 55000, "dept": "Marketing"},
    {"name": "Carol",  "age": 34, "salary": None,  "dept": "Engineering"},
    {"name": "Dave",   "age": 29, "salary": 62000, "dept": None},
    {"name": "Eve",    "age": 41, "salary": 88000, "dept": "HR"},
    {"name": "Frank",  "age": 25, "salary": 70000, "dept": "Engineering"},
    {"name": "Grace",  "age": None, "salary": 65000, "dept": "Marketing"},
    {"name": "Henry",  "age": 38, "salary": 80000, "dept": "HR"},
]

inspect_data(data)
exercises.expectedOutput:
Dataset: 8 rows

Column Info:
Column          Type       Non-Null   Null%   
---------------------------------------------
name            str        8          0.0%    
age             int        6          25.0%   
salary          int        7          12.5%   
dept            str        7          12.5%   

Numeric Column Statistics:
  age: mean=32.5  min=25  max=41
  salary: mean=70714.3  min=55000  max=88000
hints: non_null = sum(1 for v in values if v is not None)
Type: type(first_non_null).__name__ where first_non_null = next((v for v in values if v is not None), None)
For mean with non-null values: sum(values) / len(values)

exercises.title: CSV Parser (Pure Python)
exercises.description: Parse a CSV string into a list of dicts (simulating `pd.read_csv()`). Write `parse_csv(csv_text, types=None)` where `types` is an optional dict mapping column names to type conversion functions. Handle quoted fields and optional type conversion. Test with a multi-row CSV.
exercises.starterCode:
def parse_csv(csv_text, types=None):
    """
    Parse CSV text into a list of dicts.
    types: dict like {'age': int, 'salary': float} for type conversion.
    Returns list of dicts with column names as keys.
    """
    types = types or {}
    lines = csv_text.strip().split("\n")
    # TODO: Parse the first line as headers (split by ',')
    # TODO: For each subsequent line, split by ',' and pair with headers
    # TODO: Apply type conversion from types dict
    # TODO: Return list of row dicts
    pass

csv_text = """name,age,salary,department
Alice,28,75000,Engineering
Bob,32,55000,Marketing
Carol,34,82000,Engineering
Dave,29,62000,HR
Eve,41,90000,Engineering"""

records = parse_csv(csv_text, types={"age": int, "salary": int})

print(f"Loaded {len(records)} records")
print(f"Columns: {list(records[0].keys())}")
print()

# Check types were converted
print(f"age type: {type(records[0]['age']).__name__}")
print(f"salary type: {type(records[0]['salary']).__name__}")
print()

# Print summary
for r in records:
    print(f"{r['name']:<10} {r['department']:<15} ${r['salary']:,}")
exercises.expectedOutput:
Loaded 5 records
Columns: ['name', 'age', 'salary', 'department']

age type: int
salary type: int

Alice      Engineering     $75,000
Bob        Marketing       $55,000
Carol      Engineering     $82,000
Dave       HR              $62,000
Eve        Engineering     $90,000
hints: For each data line: values = line.split(','); row = dict(zip(headers, values))
Then apply types: if col in types: row[col] = types[col](row[col])
Return the list of row dicts.

exercises.title: Data Quality Report
exercises.description: Build a `DataQualityReport` that analyzes a dataset for quality issues. Check for: missing values per column, duplicate rows (by all fields), numeric columns with potential outliers (values more than 2x the mean), and constant columns (all same value). Print a formatted quality report.
exercises.starterCode:
def data_quality_report(records, label="Dataset"):
    """
    Print a data quality report for a list of dicts.
    """
    print(f"=== Data Quality Report: {label} ===")
    print(f"Rows: {len(records)}")
    print()

    columns = list(records[0].keys()) if records else []

    # TODO: 1. Missing values — count None per column
    print("Missing Values:")
    for col in columns:
        nulls = sum(1 for r in records if r[col] is None)
        if nulls > 0:
            pct = nulls / len(records) * 100
            print(f"  {col}: {nulls} missing ({pct:.1f}%)")
    # TODO: Print "  None" if no missing values found

    # TODO: 2. Duplicate rows — find exact duplicates using tuple conversion
    print("\nDuplicate Rows:")
    # TODO: Count rows where the full tuple appears more than once
    # Print count or "  None"

    # TODO: 3. Constant columns — columns where all non-null values are the same
    print("\nConstant Columns:")
    # TODO: Find columns with only 1 unique non-null value
    # Print each or "  None"

data = [
    {"id": 1, "name": "Alice",  "status": "active",   "score": 85},
    {"id": 2, "name": "Bob",    "status": "active",   "score": None},
    {"id": 3, "name": "Carol",  "status": "active",   "score": 91},
    {"id": 2, "name": "Bob",    "status": "active",   "score": None},  # duplicate
    {"id": 4, "name": "Dave",   "status": "active",   "score": 78},
    {"id": 5, "name": None,     "status": "active",   "score": 66},
]

data_quality_report(data, "Employee Scores")
exercises.expectedOutput:
=== Data Quality Report: Employee Scores ===
Rows: 6

Missing Values:
  score: 2 missing (33.3%)
  name: 1 missing (16.7%)

Duplicate Rows:
  2 duplicate rows found

Constant Columns:
  status: all values are 'active'
hints: For duplicates: convert each row to a tuple of values, then check for duplicates with Counter.
dup_count = sum(c - 1 for c in Counter(tuples).values() if c > 1)
For constant columns: non_null = [r[col] for r in records if r[col] is not None]; len(set(non_null)) == 1
Print 'None' as the string '  None' when no issues are found in a category.

warmup.title: Quick Recall: Handling None in lists
warmup.starterCode:
data = [10, None, 20, None, 30, None, 40]
non_null = [x for x in data if x is not None]
print(f"Non-null count: {len(non_null)}")
print(f"Null count: {len(data) - len(non_null)}")
print(f"Mean: {sum(non_null) / len(non_null)}")
